does: This paper proposes a hybrid defence framework for large language models that simultaneously addresses hallucination and adversarial vulnerability in natural language understanding tasks. The approach combines three complementary defensive mechanisms: entropy-based models to reduce hallucinations, uncertainty-based models to defend against adversarial attacks, and geometric-based models to enhance robustness. The authors evaluate their framework on multiple in-domain datasets (FEVER, HotpotQA, CSQA, SIQA) and out-of-distribution benchmarks (AeroEngQA, CPIQA), demonstrating substantial improvements including up to 43.34% accuracy gain on clean tasks, 64.92% improvement in adversarial robustness, 62.27% reduction in attack success rates on standard adversarial examples, and 51% reduction in attack success rates against prompt injection and jailbreak attempts.
connects: This work builds on the recognition that LLM vulnerabilities—hallucination and adversarial robustness—stem from related underlying issues in model confidence calibration and decision boundaries. It extends prior research on hallucination mitigation through entropy-based approaches and adversarial robustness methods using uncertainty quantification and geometric defences. The paper responds to the limitation of existing defences that tackle these problems in isolation, synthesizing insights from multiple defensive paradigms. It connects to broader work on robustness evaluation of NLU systems and defences against prompt-based attacks, while drawing on established techniques for confidence estimation and certified robustness in machine learning.
enables: This hybrid defence framework opens pathways for more comprehensive robustness testing of language models that accounts for multiple attack vectors simultaneously. The approach enables development of unified confidence-based mechanisms that improve both accuracy and safety without task-specific retraining, potentially applicable beyond NLU to other language tasks. The framework suggests future work on theoretically understanding the shared mechanisms underlying hallucination and adversarial vulnerability, potentially leading to provably robust defences. Results on out-of-distribution and prompt-injection datasets indicate these methods could form foundations for more reliable deployment of LLMs in safety-critical applications, while raising questions about optimal combinations of uncertainty quantification strategies for different threat models.
